coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor based on N-CDs and metal–organic frameworks for visual smart detection of malathion

coredata.dc:description: Sensitive and rapid detection of pesticide residues in food is essential for human safety. A ratiometric imprinted fluorescence sensor N-CDs@Eu-MOF@MIP (BR@MIP) was constructed to sensitively detect malathion (Mal). Europium-based metal organic frameworks (Eu-MOF) were used as supporters to improve the sensitivity of the BR@MIP. N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were used as fluorescent source to produce fluorescent signal. A linear relationship between the concentration of Mal and the fluorescence response of the sensor was found in the Mal concentration range of 1–10 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.05 μM. Furthermore, the sensor was successfully applied for the detection of Mal in lettuce, tap water, and soil samples, with recoveries in the range of 93.0 % − 99.3 %. Additionally, smartphone-based sensors were used to detect Mal in simulated real samples. Thus, the construction of ratiometric imprinted fluorescence sensor has provided a good strategy for the detection of Mal.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor based on N-CDs and metal–organic frameworks for visual smart detection of malathion

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Sensitive and rapid detection of pesticide residues in food is essential for human safety. A ratiometric imprinted fluorescence sensor N-CDs@Eu-MOF@MIP (BR@MIP) was constructed to sensitively detect malathion (Mal). Europium-based metal organic frameworks (Eu-MOF) were used as supporters to improve the sensitivity of the BR@MIP. N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were used as fluorescent source to produce fluorescent signal. A linear relationship between the concentration of Mal and the fluorescence response of the sensor was found in the Mal concentration range of 1–10 μM with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.05 μM. Furthermore, the sensor was successfully applied for the detection of Mal in lettuce, tap water, and soil samples, with recoveries in the range of 93.0 % − 99.3 %. Additionally, smartphone-based sensors were used to detect Mal in simulated real samples. Thus, the construction of ratiometric imprinted fluorescence sensor has provided a good strategy for the detection of Mal.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The organophosphorus pesticide Mal, which is a broad-spectrum insecticide and acaricide, has been widely used for the control of pests in rice, wheat, cotton and other crops. However, Mal shows the toxicity of inhibiting neurotransmitters. It also causes human poisoning through skin or mucous membrane contact, inhalation or accidental ingestion (Jokanović & Kosanović, 2010) and pollutes the water and soil in the environment (Li, Zhao, Yan, Duan, Saint, & Beecham, 2019). It is, therefore, essential to detect and accurately quantify the limited amount of Mal in food to protect the health of human. At present, the main methods for detecting Mal included gas chromatography (Lofty, Abd El-Aleem, & Monir, 2013), high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Sun, Liu, Dang, Li, Zhou, & Wang, 2012), chromatography and mass spectrometry (Valente, Tarelho, Castro, Silvestre, & Teixeira, 2015). These methods require the use of large special instruments, skilled technicians and high detection costs. On the other hand, the fluorescence analysis technique demonstrates the advantages of low cost, fast response, high sensitivity, and easy visualization. However, it has the limitation of poor selectivity.

Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) are three-dimensional network recognition materials, which are formed by the polymerization of functional monomers, crosslinkers, and initiators in the presence of target analytes (called “template molecules”) (Arabi, Ostovan, Li, Wang, Zhang, Choo, et al., 2021). The MIPs provide specific three-dimensional cavity binding sites of complementary shapes, sizes, and functions for the target analyte, which results in a high selectivity (C. Huang, Wang, Ma, Bo, Ou, & Gong, 2021). Due to the merits of excellent specificity, high selectivity and simplicity of synthesis (W. Hu, Feng, Pei, Du, Liu, Mu, et al., 2023), the MIPs have been used in the fields of sensing (Javanbakht, Fard, Mohammadi, Abdouss, Ganjali, Norouzi, et al., 2008), environmental science (Pichon & Chapuis-Hugon, 2008), food safety (Basak, Venkatram, & Singhal, 2022), and biomedicine (da Silva, Viveiros, Morgado, Aguiar-Ricardo, Correia, & Casimiro, 2011). Recently, ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensors have large gained attention, which is due to their excellent sensitivity, self-calibration properties (Gong, Zhang, Zhang, Luo, Fu, Tan, et al., 2012), and sufficient color variation (Y.-Q. Wang, Zhao, He, Li, & Zhang, 2014). Huang et al. (M. Huang & Tong, 2022) constructed an imprinted ratiometric fluorescence probe for a sensitive and selective detection of tetracycline and ferric ions. Jalili et al. . (Jalili & Khataee, 2020) fabricated a mesoporous-structured ratiometric imprinted sensor for sensitive visual detection of chloramphenicol. Currently, several studies have reported on the use of the imprinted fluorescence method for the detection of organophosphorus (such as omethoate (S. Huang, Guo, Tan, Geng, Wu, Tang, et al., 2018), trichlorfon (Yang Liu, Cao, Gui, & Ma, 2018), and methamidophos (X. Liu, Liu, Kong, Qiao, & Xu, 2018)). However, there are no reports on Mal detection using N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and molecular imprinting techniques. Besides, most of the reported fluorescence imprinted sensors for organophosphorus rely on the single-channel fluorescence response, which is susceptible to interferences from instrumentation, environment, and probe concentration (Han, Fan, Qing, Liu, Yang, Li, et al., 2020). On the contrary, a ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor with dual channels can provide a built-in signal self-calibration, which facilitates more sensitive and reliable analytical results in practical applications (W. Hu, Pei, Feng, Du, Liu, Mu, et al., 2023; Jalili, Khataee, Rashidi, & Luque, 2019; H. Zhu, Zhang, Zhang, & Wang, 2012).

The development of luminescent materials is correlated with the advancements in ratiometric fluorescent imprinted sensor development (Guo, Chen, Yu, & Wang, 2021). Metal organic frameworks (MOFs) have been constructed by using the self-assembling of organic ligands and inorganic metal ions (or clusters) through coordination bonds. The MOFs possess extremely large surface areas, uniform pore sizes, powerful chemical tailoring, and high thermal stabilities, and they have been used for the creation of fluorescence (Tang, Zhao, Wu, Chen, Tang, Zhou, et al., 2022). Due to the elasticity of the MOF composition, the MOFs have also been used for gas adsorption/storage (Moon, Kim, & Suh, 2005), heterogeneous catalysis (Llewellyn, Bourrelly, Serre, Filinchuk, & Férey, 2006), luminescence (El-Sewify, Shenashen, Shahat, Yamaguchi, Selim, Khalil, et al., 2017), and carrier functions (Yaghi, 2007). In addition, the MOFs can be used as nanomaterial scaffolds loaded with organic probes for the design of optical and fluorescent chemical sensors for heavy metal detection (El-Sewify, Shenashen, Shahat, Selim, Khalil, & El-Safty, 2018). Furthermore, it must be noted that the lanthanide metal organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs) demonstrate outstanding luminescence properties. In fact, they showed promising potential for sensing applications (X. Hu, Guo, Wang, Liu, Yang, & Fang, 2022). At present, some ratiometric fluorescence sensors based on lanthanide elements, especially europium ion (Eu3+) have been used for the detection of tetracycline antibiotics (Zhang, Wang, Jia, Bi, Bie, Chen, et al., 2021). Also, the prepared Ln-MOFs-based ratiometric fluorescence sensors have been combined with the luminescent properties of nanoparticles with those of the Ln-MOFs. This combination allowed for superior performance, as compared with the individual components (Qin & Yan, 2018). Moreover, carbon dots (CDs) with attractive optical properties, physicochemical stabilities, biocompatibilities, and low toxicities (Molaei, 2020), have been encapsulated in luminescence metal organic frameworks (LMOFs). This encapsulation brought more prominent advantages to the sensors (Amiripour, Ghasemi, & Azizi, 2021). However, the ratiometric fluorescence sensors still showed the disadvantage of poor portability for practical applications, which is not advantageous for the real-time and on-site detection of malathion. It is, therefore, an urgent task to develop a portable device for the real-time detection of Mal.

Smartphones have the advantages of easy operation, small size, fast processing speed, and high resolution. These properties are useful in the field of sensing (L. Yang, Hu, Pei, Du, Tong, Mu, et al., 2024), offering a portable monitoring platform for real-time and on-site detection (L. Wang, Wen, Chen, Wang, & Li, 2022). Currently, a series of smartphone-based fluorescence sensing platforms have been constructed and applied for the detection of various analytes. For example, Wu et al. (Wu, Zhao, Tang, Chen, Tang, Lei, et al., 2023) developed a smartphone-based fluorescence sensing platform for the real-time detection of homovanillic acid. Also Zhu et al. (X. Zhu, Han, Liu, & Sun, 2022) performed a the selective and sensitive detection of pyrethroids by using a smartphone-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor.

In this work, a novel ratiometric fluorescence imprinted sensor (BR@MIP), based on europium-based metal organic framework (Eu-MOF) and N-CDs, has been constructed for the recognition of Mal. The red luminescence source (Eu-MOF) and blue quantum dots (N-CDs) were then used as reference and response signals, respectively. The Eu-MOF, as supporters and reference signal, boosted mass transfer and sensitivity, and the MIPs on the surface of the Eu-MOF acted as recognition sites for Mal. Due to the combination of Eu-MOF, N-CDs, and MIPs, the prepared BR@MIP fluorescence sensor exhibited an excellent visualization, sensitivity, and selectivity for Mal. Furthermore, to fulfill the needs for visual detection and portable strategies for Mal detection, a dexterous and inexpensive smartphone-assisted fluorescence sensing device was prepared. It was applied for Mal detection in lettuce, tap water, and soil with satisfactory recoveries. This work offered a strategy for the construction of MOF-based ratiometric fluorescence sensors.

Citric acid, ethanediamine, methanol, ethanol and N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Aladdin Regents Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Also, europium nitrate (Eu(NO3)3), 5-boronobenzene-1,3-dicarboxylic acid (BBDC), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS, 99.0 %), 3-aminopropyrtriethoxysilane (APTES, 98.0 %), ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O, 25 %), malathion, thiamethoxam, imidacloprid, diazinon, phoxophos, glyphosate, dimethoate, and chlorpyrifos were purchased from Adamas Reagent Co., Ltd. Deionized water was used for all the experimental procedures, and All chemical reagents were of analytical grade without the need for further purification.

Fluorescence analyses were performed with an FLS1000 spectrophotometer (Thermal Scientific, USA). Also, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy analyses were performed by using PerkinElmer Nicolet IS50 (USA). Furthermore, the ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) adsorption spectra were measured by using a Biomate 3S UV–Visible Spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA), and X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses were performed using BRUCKER D8 ADVANCE (Bruker, Germany). In addition, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using Tecnai G2 S-TWIN (Thermo Scientific, USA), and the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained from ESCALAB 250Xi (Thermo Scientific, USA).

N-CDs were prepared (Ren & Fan, 2023) according to the previous method. 10 g of citric acid was then dissolved in 40 mL of ultra-pure water, and 5 mL of ethylenediamine was, thereafter, gradually added to this solution. The final solution was then placed in an autoclave and heated to 200 ℃ for 5 h. Finally, the obtained brown solution was purified by dialysis to obtain an N-CDs solution for further use.

Eu-MOF was synthesized according to the previous method (X. Hu, Guo, Zhang, Wang, Fang, & Wang, 2022). Eu(NO3)3 (0.4 mmol) and BBDC (0.4 mmol) were then mixed with 12 mL of water and 28 mL of DMF, and vigorously stirred at room temperature for 2 h. The mixture was, thereafter, moved to an autoclave and heated to 130 ℃ for 12 h. After cooling to 25 ℃, the obtained white solution was purified by centrifugation, and then washed with DMF and ethanol to remove the unreacted substances. The white Eu-MOFs powder was, finally, obtained by drying overnight.

N-CDs@Eu-MOF@MIP (BR@MIP) was prepared by using a modified sol–gel method. As-synthesized Eu-MOF (20 mg) and N-CDs (50 μl) were first dispersed in 5 mL of a phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH = 7.0, 10 mM). In, addition, 10 mg of malathion was dissolved in 1 mL of PBS. These two solutions were then mixed, and 100 μl of APTES was, thereafter, added. After stirring for 1 h, TEOS (120 μl) and NH3·H2O (120 μl) were sequentially added to the solution, and it was allowed to react at 25 ℃ for 12 h. As the next step, the coarse product was centrifuged and repeatedly washed with a methanol solution until no template could be detected under UV–Vis irradiation (220 nm) in the supernatant solution. The BR@MIP product was finally obtained by freeze-drying. Morever, the non-imprinted polymer N-CDs@Eu-MOF@NIP (BR@NIP) was prepared by using the same procedure as for BR@MIP, except for the addition of the template molecule. For comparison purposes, the contrast material N-CDs@MIP was synthesized by the same procedure. This was also the situation with the synthesis of Eu-MOF@MIP, except for the addition of N-CDs.

The fluorescence measurement of Mal was performed by using fluorescence spectrometry with a photomultiplier voltage of 950 V, scanning step of 2 nm, and slit width of 3.0 nm. The BR@MIP solution was then mixed with different concentrations of malathion in a tube, and the final concentrations of BR@MIP in the tubes were set to 1.0 mg/mL. Different concentrations of malathion were added into the BR@MIP solution for 1 min (V/V 1:1). The fluorescence spectra of the solutions in these tubes were, thereafter, measured in the range of 410–750 nm at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Control experiments with BR@NIP were also performed with same steps.

In the present study, a 3D printing device was designed and produced to avoid interference from light sources and other factors. The main components of this device are shown in Fig. S9. A 365 nm LED lamp (3 W) equipped with a portable charging case was then fixed at an appropriate position as an excitation light source. A filter (400 nm) was also placed between the sample tank and the smartphone to eliminate any interference from the excitation beam. Finally, fluorescent color images were extracted by using the smartphone, and converted to RGB values using a specialized software (Color Picker app).

To assess potential practicability of BR@MIP, the tap water, lettuce and soil were selected as simulated real samples in the evaluation of the potential applicability of the BR@MIP. The lettuce was purchased from a local supermarket. It was first washed with water and then spiked with 1 mL of different concentrations of Mal standard solutions (1 μM, 5 μM, and 10 μM). After being dried at room temperature for 6 h, the spiked samples were cut into small pieces. 25 mL of methanol–water mixture (6:4, v/v) was added and the final solution was sonicated for 30 min. The mixture was then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. As the next step, the obtained supernatant solution was filtered by a 0.22 μm membrane. To remove solid suspensions, the soil samples were filtered through qualitative filter membranes with pore sizes of 0.22 µm. They were, thereafter, centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min. Furthermore, the tap water was directly used without any pretreatment. The samples of the lettuce, soil, and tap water, with different spiked concentrations of malathion (1 μM, 5 μM, and 10 μM), were then added to the BR@MIP, and the fluorescence spectra of all these samples were immediately recorded by the fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The construction of the dual emission ratiometric fluorescence sensor was depicted in Scheme 1 . The Eu-MOF, which was used for a reference signal, was prepared from 5-boronobenzene-1,3-dicarboxylic acid (BBDC) and europium nitrate by the one-pot method. The N-CDs, which were used for the response signal, showed a strong blue fluorescence. The Eu-MOF provided a favorable support for the encapsulation of the N-CDs and for the formation of Mal imprinting sites. The adsorption capacity of the nanosensor was, thereby, improved and a signal amplification was realized. In addition, the silicon-based imprinted layer acted as a recognition unit, which improved the anti-interference ability of the nanosensor. In short, the fluorescence nanosensor integrated the advantages with the N-CDs, Eu-MOF, and imprinted layer, thereby offering a new strategy for a fast and efficient detection of Mal. Furthermore, the molecularly imprinted polymers were synthesized by using the sol–gel method together with Mal, 3-aminopropyrtriethoxysilane (APTES), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), N-CDs, and Eu-MOF. After elution, the BR@MIP acquired specific identification cavities matching the Mal in size and shape. In the presence of malathion, the blue fluorescence of N-CDs (at 430 nm) was effectively enhanced by a nonradiative recombination, which was triggered by the defective states of the CDs surfaces. However, the red fluorescence of Eu-MOF (at 660 nm) was unchanged. Thus, an efficient dual-channel strategy enabled the BR@MIP ratiometric fluorescence sensor to display a fluorescence color change from red to blue, resulting in sensitive and visual detection of Mal.

TEM was utilized to investigated the morphologies and structures of N-CDs, Eu-MOF, BR@MIP and BR@NIP. The diameters of N-CDs were about 4 nm with a good dispersion (Fig. S1). Also, the Eu-MOF microcrystallines were spherical, with an average size of 500 nm (Fig. 1 A). As illustrated in Fig. 1B and C, the average sizes of BR@MIP and BR@NIP were about 600 nm. The size of the imprinted polymer was larger than that of Eu-MOF, which demonstrated the successful preparation of imprinted polymer. The elements mapping (Fig. 1D) and EDS spectrum (Fig. S2) demonstrated that the characteristic elements (N, C, Eu, B, Si) were contained in the imprinted polymers. These results manifested that the BR@MIP was successfully synthesized.

XRD was used to verify the crystal structures of Eu-MOF, BR@MIP, and BR@NIP. The XRD pattern of Eu-MOF was portrayed in Fig. 1E, and were consistent with the reported reference (Cui, Chen, & Yin, 2019). Additionally, the crystallinities of the BR@MIP have changed. Moreover, as illustrated in Fig. S2, the results of EDS spectrum further manifested the synthesis of BR@MIP. These results displayed successful synthesis of the imprinted polymer layer.

The surface functional groups of Eu-MOF, BR@MIP, and BR@NIP were analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy. As illustrated in Fig. 1F, the peak of Eu-MOF at 1389 cm−1 corresponded to the vibration of B-O (Z.-R. Yang, Wang, Wang, & Yin, 2017). The peaks at 3320 cm−1 and 1656 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of OH/NH and bending vibration of NH, respectively, indicating that N-CDs was embedded in the Eu-MOF (Shu, Ye, Dai, Xu, & Hu, 2021). The characteristic peak at 2966 cm−1 was ascribed to the antisymmetric stretching vibration of CH, and because of the presence of aminopropyl of APTES, the peak at 1056 cm−1 was associated with the asymmetric Si-O-Si stretching. The peaks at 781 cm−1 and 448 cm−1 belonged to the Si-O vibrations and Si-O-Si bending vibrations, respectively (J. Yang, Feng, Liang, Chen, & Cai, 2020). These results demonstrated triumphant formation of the imprinted silica layer and synthesis of BR@MIP.

As illustrated in Fig. 2 , The XPS of N-CDs was investigated. The XPS spectra of C1s showed three peaks at 284.1 eV, 285.2 eV, and 287.6 eV that belonged to CC/CC, CO and CO, respectively (Fig. 2B). The XPS spectra of N1s showed three peaks at 399.7 eV, 400.7 eV, and 401.5 eV ( Fig. 2C), which were associated with NC, NH, and NO respectively (Ren & Fan, 2023). The XPS spectra of O1s showed two peaks at 530.4 eV and 532.3 eV that were ascribed to the CO and CO vibrations, respectively ( Fig. 2D). These results denoted that the CDs were successfully doped with N.

Since the imprinted efficiency affected the sensor performance, the APTES dosage and TEOS dosage have here been optimized (Fig. S3A and S3B). 100 μl of APTES and 120 μl of TEOS were then used for subsequent experiments. It was found that the acidity and alkalinity affected the imprinting effect, and the fluorescence quenching efficiency was highest at pH = 7.0 (Fig. S3C). A pH value of 7.0 was, therefore, used for the subsequent experiments. The fluorescence enhancement efficiency of the BR@MIP reached stability within only 1.0 min (Fig. S3D). The above phenomena were caused by the larger surface area of the Eu-MOF. Moreover, the fluorescence enhancement efficiency of the BR@MIP, which was attributed to the specific imprinted sites for Mal detection, was higher than that of the BR@NIP. As can be seen in Fig. S3E, the fluorescence intensity of the BR@MIP was strongest at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Furthermore, for the improvement of the fluorescence signal readout, a ratio of 50 μl:20 mg (N-CDs:Eu-MOF) was used in the synthesis of the BR@MIP (Fig. S3F). Also, the imprinting time affected the adsorption capacity of the as-prepared BR@MIP. This was achieved by adjusting the thickness of the imprinting layer. As can be seen in Fig. S4A, the optimal reaction time was 12 h. Furthermore, the dosage of the used BR@MIPs was found to be closely related to the fluorescence response, which was crucial for the improvement of the sensitivity of the quantitative analysis and the linear range of the fluorescence sensor. (C.-X. Liu, Zhao, Zhang, Zhang, Xu, Sun, et al., 2020). As can be seen in Fig. S4B, 2 mg/ml BR@MIPs was then used as the optimal dosage for the sensor.

As can be seen in Fig. S5A, the fluorescence intensity was unchanged after the addition of Mal to the Eu-MOF. In addition, the fluorescence response of the Eu-MOF imprinted material (Eu-MOF@MIP) to Mal was also studied (Fig. S5B). As a result, the fluorescence intensity became almost unchanged after the addition of 5 μM of Mal. Thus, it was found that Mal was unresponsive to the Eu-MOF, thereby failing to enhance the luminescence. Instead, carbon dots were found to play a key role in the luminescence behavior of the BR@MIP.

Currently, the proposed photoluminescence mechanisms include the surface state, quantum confinement effect, molecular state, synergistic effect, and conjugation effect (Ai, Yang, Wang, Chang, Tang, Yang, et al., 2021). In fact, the surface states of the CDs play a more important role in their luminescence behavior. For example, some traps and nonradiative relaxation processes on the surfaces of the CDs can reduce the fluorescence signal. This is due to the presence of defective states, which is attributed to the presence of certain groups on the surface of the CDs, such as alkylamines and carbonyl groups. These groups can trigger a nonradiative recombination and, thus, reduce the luminescence efficiency of the CDs. Passivation or reduction of these groups can, therefore, restore the fluorescence signal of the CDs (Zheng, Wang, Long, Zhang, Huang, & Zhu, 2011; S. Zhu, Wang, Zhou, Zhao, Song, Maharjan, et al., 2014). The fluorescence signal of the N-CDs was significantly weaker than that of the BR@MIP (Fig. S5C). This was attributed to the thicker imprinted silicon layer on the N-CDs, which could mask the fluorescence of the internal N-CDs. This was explanation for the weaker fluorescence signal of the BR@MIP (Yixi Liu, Liu, He, He, & Ma, 2016; Long, Li, Tong, Wu, & Shi, 2019; L. Wang, Wen, Zhao, Chao, Tao, Wang, et al., 2022). Also, the presence of alkylamine groups in APTES induced the nonradiative nature of the excited electrons in the N-CDs, which led to the attenuation of the fluorescence signal of the BR@MIP. However, the fluorescence signal of the BR@MIP was found to be gradually restored in the presence of Mal (Fig. S5D). The reason was that the alkylamine group of APTES could be passivated by the interaction with Mal, thereby reducing the occurrence of nonradiative recombinations. In addition, the fluorescence signal of N-CDs in the BR@MIP was gradually recovered (Kazemifard, Ensafi, & Rezaei, 2020; L. Wang, et al., 2022; S. Zhu, Song, Zhao, Shao, Zhang, & Yang, 2015). In contrast, since there was no molecularly imprinted cavity on the BR@NIP, only a small amount of Mal binds to APTES through nonspecific binding, which resulted in a slight recovery of the fluorescence signal of the BR@NIP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of a fluorescence sensing system
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission curves of BR@MIP and BR@NIP for various concentrations of Mal are shown in Fig. 3 A and B for optimal conditions. When the concentration of Mal increased from 0 μM to 10 μM, the fluorescence at 430 nm gradually increased, while the fluorescence at 616 nm remained unchanged. However, the enhancement effect of the BR@MIP was more pronounced compared to the effect of the BR@NIP. It was attributed to the presence of specific imprinting sites on the surface of the BR@MIP. These sites matched the functional group, structure, and size of the Mal. On the contrary, there were no imprinting sites on the surface of the BR@NIP. Furthermore, the Stern Volmer plots of BR@MIP and BR@NIP are shown in Fig. 3C and D, respectively. There was an almost linear relationship for the fluorescence enhancement efficiency (F430/F616)/(F430/F616)0 versus concentration for the BR@MIP in the concentration range of 1 μM−10 μM, with a correlation coefficient of 0.995. The corresponding equation for the BR@MIP was found to be y = 0.4927x + 0.9991, while the linear equation for the BR@NIP was y = 0.1186x + 0.4973. Also, the imprinting factor (IF) was used to evaluate the imprinting effect of the BR@MIP. The IF was 4.15 according to report (Fu, Zhou, Zhao, Wu, Tang, Chen, et al., 2022), which proved that the BR@MIP had a high recognition ability for Mal detection. In addition, according to the function LOD = 3σ/k, the detection limit for Mal reached 0.05 μM. Furthermore, an obvious color change, from red to blue, could be observed for irradiation with a UV light at 365 nm. This observation was caused by the intensity change of the two clearly resolved emission peaks, making it possible to detect Mal by the naked eye. As can be seen in Fig. 3, the BR@MIP demonstrated a more pronounced fluorescence color change than the BR@NIP, which stemmed from the presence of imprinted sites on the BR@MIP for Mal detection.

For comparison, N-CDs have also been used as fluorescent probes to detect single signals of Mal. As the concentration of Mal increased, the fluorescence of the N-CDs became progressively enhanced (Fig. S6). The linear equation for the fluorescence enhancement efficiency ((F430)/(F430)0) versus Mal concentration could be expressed as y = 0.0850x + 1.0759, with an R2 value of 0.991. In addition, the LOD value was 0.5 μM. As compared with the single signal sensing based on N-CDs, the N-CDs/Eu-MOF-based ratiometric fluorescence method showed a wider linear range and higher sensitivity, which may be attributed to the self-correction property of the ratiometric fluorescence. More importantly, the ratiometric fluorescence method based on N-CDs/Eu-MOF showed notable color variations for different concentrations of Mal, whereas the single-signal sensing based on N-CDs only showed brightness variations for Mal. These results clearly proved that the ratiometric fluorescence method that was based on N-CDs/Eu-MOF had a significant advantage over the single fluorescence response.

To evaluate the anti-interference ability of the BR@MIP, some potential interferers such as K+, Ca2+, Na+, Cl-, Cu2+, SO4 2-, Pb2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, NO3 –, Ni+, and Co2+ ions have been investigated (Fig. S7A). The fluorescence response ((F430/F616)/(F430/F616)0) of the BR@MIP was obviously higher than those of the other interferers. Thus, the results showed that these interferers could not affect the recognition of Mal by the BR@MIP. In addition, the selectivity of the BR@MIP for malathion, thiamethoxam, imidacloprid, diazinon, phoxophos, glyphosate, dimethoate, and chlorpyrifos was studied by measuring the (F430/F616)/(F430/F616)0 values (Fig. S7B). The fluorescence response of the BR@MIP for Mal detection was also here higher than for the other substances. This was due to the fact that the specific recognition sites on BR@MIP were complementary to Mal in terms of size, shape, and functional group. The results showed also that the BR@MIP had a high selectivity.

The stability is an important factor for the BR@MIP, and has here been studied by measuring the fluorescence response of the BR@MIP. The fluorescence stability of the BR@MIP did not remarkably change during the first 20 days (Fig. S8A), demonstrating a high stability. Moreover, eight samples were also chosen for a study of the repeatability of the BR@MIP. The changes in fluorescence response were then monitored (Fig. S8B). The results showed that the BR@MIP had excellent repeatability. An estimation of the reusability of the BR@MIP was also made, where five removal-rebinding cycles were performed. As can be seen in Fig. S8c Supporting information, as the number of repetitions increased, the ((F430/F616)/(F430/F616)0) value decreased slightly and remained constant at 96.78 % of the initial value. Thus, the results showed that the BR@MIP possessed good reusability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of malathion in lettuce, tap water, and soil
ce:section.ce:para: Lettuce, tap water, and soil were used as simulated real samples in the validation of the applicability of the BR@MIP ratiometric fluorescence sensor. Different concentrations of Mal (1 μM, 5 μM, and 10 μM) were added to the samples for recovery assessment, and the experimental results are shown in Table 1 . The recoveries ranged from 93.0 % to 99.3 %. These results indicated that the BR@MIP ratiometric fluorescence sensor was an effective method for the detection of Mal in real samples. Similarly, lettuce, soil and tap water were used to investigate the practicality of the smartphone-based sensing platform. The recovery rates were 91.6-98.2% with RSD < 3.1%, as shown in Table 2 . The results indicated that the sensing platform achieved satisfactory results on the detection of Mal and this method has great potential for onsite detection of Mal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted fluorescence sensing for real-time Mal detection
ce:section.ce:para: A 3D printed device, which consisted of an optical filter, UV lamp, sample tank, lightbox, and smartphone, was prepared for on-site detection of Mal (Fig. S9). The samples contained different concentrations of Mal and MIP, and were placed in cuvettes. Fluorescence images were, thereafter, obtained by taking pictures with a smartphone. They were then converted to RGB values by the Color picker software. The B/R showed a good linear relationship with the Mal concentration in the range of 2 μM−7 μM. This relationship was expressed as y = 0.1882x + 0.3166 (R2 = 0.984), with a detection limit of 1.45 μΜ. In addition, the BR@MIP showed a more profound color change (from pink to blue) than the BR@NIP (Fig. S10). These results showed that the constructed sensing equipment offered a reliable strategy for on-site detection of Mal.

In conclusion, a ratiometric imprinted fluorescence sensor (BR@MIP) has in the present study been successfully prepared to selectively identify and sensitively detect malathion (Mal). The presence of defect states on the surfaces of the CDs triggered the non-radiative recombination, which in turn attenuated the luminescence efficiency of the CDs. In addition, the interaction of Mal with APTES enhanced the fluorescence signal of the sensing system. Furthermore, the dual emission-based fluorescence sensing improved the sensitivity and accuracy of the Mal detection. Also, the established BR@MIP showed good selectivity and satisfactory reusability. A good practical application of the fluorescence sensor was finally achieved by the detection of Mal in lettuce, tap water, and soil samples. In addition, a portable optical sensing platform was successfully developed for on-site analysis of Mal. The visual sensing of Mal was achieved by using a smartphone, thereby reducing the analysis time and cost. Furthermore, the distinct change in fluorescent color of the sensing system (from red to blue) facilitated a visual analysis. Thus, the present study has provided a strategy for the construction of ratiometric fluorescence sensors, and has also provided a feasible method for Mal detection.
